speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape six. The school that you were telling us about that you and your husband built, what kind of a school was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a religious school. While we established our life together in America, we decided that we will follow exactly the rules the way we used to live and the way we brought up-- religious, modern religious, keep everything to the Torah, and help religious organizations. And that's exactly what we did. We brought our five children Orthodox, our grandchildren. Our great-grandchildren, I hope they will be. And we had a very strict, very modern, Orthodox home. And I-- I'm still continuing to have the same thing, even though my husband passed away 12 years ago. And my children are continuing in the same way.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your children. What are their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I have two wonderful children. Margaret Esther Rapaport married Retter, R-E-T-T-E-R, and Michael-- Martin Michael Rapaport, my son. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] My daughter has a beautiful family, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] four children-- Lea Retter, Avi Retter, Yael Retter, and Aaron Retter. Lea Retter is married. Her married name is Schiff-- S-C-H-- S-C-H-I-F-F. She has the most beautiful, adorable, three little children who are they my great-grandchildren. Their name is Philip, Ruhama, and Leora. My son, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] who recently-- two years ago-- moved to Israel and has [INAUDIBLE] 10 beautiful children, six boys and four girls. Before I tell their name, I have to mention something here. When he got married, he said, Mommy, Daddy, one thing god promise-- I'm going to have lots of children. And I will put back the family again. My grandchildrens' name is Mordechai, Israel, Arje, Miriam, Shimson, Leah, Nina, Elijahu, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Mayer, and Bracha. Those are the ten Rapaport children, who at the moment settled in Israel. Two years ago, they went to Israel. They love it. And I thank my son and [INAUDIBLE] to be able to put back the Rapaport name and our family.

speaker: interviewer
text: When your son decided to go to Israel, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] did he do it because he is religious?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, all my children and grandchildren are religious. But when my husband and I came out to America, we very much wanted to go and visit Israel and see Israel. And we were very much-- we became very much Zionist and very much for Israel. We had special feelings, because when we went through, we felt that a Israeli state is very, very important.

speaker: interviewee
text: So actually, every year, from the moment we could afford it, we went to visit Israel. And when my children were small, this is where we spent on vacations I kept taking them there every year. And we spent a lot of time there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, when my son became bar mitzvah, after bar mitzvah, he asked us we should let him go to Israel to study. And my husband and I did. He went to a yeshiva, to Israel, and studied there for two years. And then he begged us to let him stay another year. And I said, I'm very sorry. You have to go to university. And you have to further your education. And then he said, may I please do that in Bar-Ilan university. And we agreed.

speaker: interviewee
text: So for seven years, he stayed in Israel. He went to Bar-Ilan University. And he loved Israel truly and truly. And he always said, one day, I'm going to come back to live here. And this is how it happened. When he went back to Israel, did he take anything with him from your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was one very, very precious thing what my husband found. That was a Sefer Torah what was put away in the well. It was my husband's grandfather's.

speaker: interviewer
text: Put away in a well where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Romania, in his home town-- in his home, there was a very deep, dry well. And that's where they packed it up and put it. And when he came home, he got it out. And they brought it to America. When my husband passed away, I gave this to my son. Because I felt he is the one who has that habit. When he went to Israel, that time he had nine children. The tenth was born already in Israel. He took the Sefer Torah with him. And people say it's a very big [INAUDIBLE] to go to Israel, to be able to take the Sefer Torah, which was his grandfather's. And also, it's a big miracle for the Sefer Torah to escape and survive and wound up in Israel, in Jerusalem with my son, his wife, who is a wonderful girl, the and the 10 children.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned three great-grandchildren.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They live in Belgium. This is my first granddaughter from my daughter, whose name is Lea Retter Schiff, married the wonderful boy who is also religious. And I have three wonderful, wonderful great-grandchildren. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Last week, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I had the honor to become the fourth time and a great-grandmother from my daughter's son, whose name is Avi Retter, whose wife gave him a beautiful ni-- eight, practically eight pound big son, whose name is Mayer [INAUDIBLE], Mayer, really. And God gave me the honor that I was able to be there at the bris. Thank you, God.

speaker: interviewer
text: What effect have your experiences during the war had on the way you have lived your life since and the way you raised your children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, during the war, during the camp years, the only way you were really able to survive if you had a very strong belief, which I always had. I never gave up. And perhaps that helped me survive, besides that I was perhaps a young and healthy girl. But there were many young and healthy girls. Never gave up, and I had a belief. Once I came out of the camp, I survived and my husband survived-- he also live by the same belief. When we established our life, we decided to continue exactly in the same spirit, the same way, the same religious way, the way we were brought up, our parents and great-grandparents lived. And we brought up our children modern religious way. We taught them that religion is beautiful. It holds the family together. And belief is one of the most important things in life. It gives you strength. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Thank God my children, my grandchildren, my great-grandchildren, live in this spirit. My daughter is a lawyer. My son-in-law is a wonderful man who is also a lawyer-- modern, intelligent, but he learns every day. He's religious. He's a well-educated, secular and religious ways. And the same goes for my son, , who is a businessman, and my daughter-in-law, who is the most wonderful girl, brings the children up religiously. They never miss the morning prayers in shul. My son takes them whenever he is home from his business trips. And they never miss a Friday night or a Sabbath that they shouldn't can go and pray. Matter of fact, my son, every Friday night, takes his boys to the Kotel to say his prayers. And whoever doesn't have where to eat, brings them home to his Shabbat table. Sometimes with the 10 children and them, they have five, six, seven, and eight extra people eating there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you ever told your son that your father did the same thing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did. I did during the war. I never spoke to them much about my life in the camp, because I didn't want them to feel, oh, poor mother. But I'm glad to make this tape. Because when the time comes and I have to go, this is what I'm going to leave for them. And then they will know the story of my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you ever have any dreams of the war years?

speaker: interviewee
text: I used to have a lot. It's less and less, unless I really talk about it. But forget, never-- everything is-- every day and every episode is very vivid in my mind. But I don't like to talk about my sorrows, my hardship, to my children. I never want them to feel that poor mother, what she had to suffer. Thank God I had the most wonderful life. I had the most wonderful husband who gave us the most wonderful life. And I was able to bring up my children, giving them the best, the finest in living and education and everything there is in life. And I'm very proud of it. I never want them ever, ever to feel that because they are Jewish, because they are religious, they are second class citizens. They are as good as anyone in this world. That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So many people did not survive. Why do you think you were able to survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Three reasons-- first of all, because I was young. Second of all, I had a very strong belief. And third of all, I am sure my mother kept a helping hand on me. Because I saw so many miracles, so many incidents which wasn't normal, that there must been somebody above there watching go over me. And I'm sure it's my mother. It was my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you decide to tell your story at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] first of all, I'm old enough. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You never know how long life will last. I did want to leave something for my children. Second of all, if my children will hear the story now, they will understand much more. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what can I tell you? It's right. I realized that it's right to leave something behind. People should know it from those who really were there who really-- it's not a story. This is life. You just call it a story. But this is my life, the way it really happened. Everything I said I lived through. It's not something like writing a book and you write the story. This is life. This was my life. And perhaps at this time-- and I thank Spielberg for being so wonderful and keep this alive that the people should remember forever and ever. And never, ever anybody should think that they are superior. Nobody is superior. And you can't kill our people because they think they're better. We are all God's creation. The only way you can be more than an other if you educate yourself, and if you have a good heart, and if you help the others to survive. Thank you, Spielberg. You're wonderful. I know your mother. And she's the most wonderful, sweetest person in this world also. Every time I'm in LA, I always visit her. And we have a wonderful time together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us your number?

speaker: interviewee
text: 6684-- my lucky, surviving number.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you get that number?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got this in Auschwitz-Birkenau before I started working there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Reflection. Who's on the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my father, Eugene Pasztor, after World War I was-- this picture must have been taken approximately 1918 or '19.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in Satoraljaujhely. This is where he came back from the war. He was wounded. And, and this is where he met my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is on it?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my mother and father after my father got well from the wounds of the war, of the First World War. They got married. They got married in Budapest approximately 1922, '23.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what are their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: There names are Marget-- Margaret Pasztor P-A-S-T-O-R. Her maiden name was Ehrman-- E-R, E-H-R-M-A-N. And my father's name is Eugene Pasztor, P-A-S-Z-T-O-R-.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this little old girl who's picture survived is Susie Pasztor Rapaport. This is me at the age of around four years old in 1929.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Satoraljaujhely. [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is it?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my brother and I. This picture was taken in approximately when I was about 12, 13 years old. And he was about eight, nine years old, in 1935?

speaker: interviewer
text: What is his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: 30-something. His name is Steven Pasztor. And this was just before the bad things had happened to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is there?

speaker: interviewee
text: In [INAUDIBLE]. In Satoraljaujhely. This was sent out to some relatives to America. And this is how the picture survived. Unfortunately, my brother did not survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. [INAUDIBLE]. Who is it?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the only family picture what I have where I have my grandmother, who was Lea Ehrman, my mother's mother. And I have my mother there, Margaret Pasztor. I have my brother there, Steven Pasztor. And I have my uncle, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] also my aunt, and their children. This picture was taken in 19-- approximately in 1940, '41, perhaps, in Satoraljaujhely. I must tell you there are two girls on the right hand side, who survived the concentration camp and unfortunately died in [? cellar ?] whose names are Ava and Clara Ehrman. I am the only survivor out of this picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my husband, Leo Rapaport. This picture was taken approximately 1948, '49, in New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's good.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the document which my husband got after he was liberated to make it official that he was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: The year-- he was there till 1945. But this document is dated 19-- 1945 it was [? dated. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: And where is it? What country?

speaker: interviewee
text: This comes from Camp Feldafing. This is where he was liberated.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Stalag, they brought him there. And this is where he was liberated.

speaker: interviewer
text: What country?

speaker: interviewee
text: By the Americans.

speaker: interviewer
text: What country?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Very--

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Mr. and Mrs. Leo Rapaport. This is the picture of my husband and I after we came to America. Says we don't have a wedding picture because that time we got-- when we got married, it was right after the war. We couldn't afford a wedding when we came to America. And things got better, we decided to take our picture. It was taken approximately 1948. OK? Anniversary. This is my lovely family, the wedding of my daughter, which was taken in 1966-- my husband, Leo Rapaport, me, Susan Rapaport, my wonderful daughter, Margaret Rapaport, and my wonderful son, Martin Rapaport. This was a wedding reception for my daughter, who got married to Daniel Retter, also a wonderful, wonderful boy.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in the New York Americana, which that time was built. And-- This is my daughter, her husband, and family-- Mr and Mrs. Retter-- Daniel Retter, Margaret Retter, Lea Retter, Avi Retter, Yael Retter, and Aaron Retter. This picture was taken in 1976, the occasion of the grandson's, Avi's bar mitzvah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In New York City.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's good. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Wait. Where was th-- this is my son and my daughter-in-law, Martin Michael Rapaport and Rivka Rapaport. This picture was taken 1985 at a reception in New York City. Mr. And Mrs. Martin Rapaport and family carrying the Sefer Torah of his great-grandfather's, coming to Aliyah in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1994, Jerusalem. My two granddaughters on Yael's wedding day, and the Schiff family, my great-grandchildren, the Schiffs.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year? Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1996. Yael just get married.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is it? What is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, this is one of our school buildings in Jerusalem in honor of my son's bar mitzvah in 1965, '66, one of the buildings. We have two more. One is in Akko. And one is in Muncie, New York.
